text:
From BI to AI:Making Data-Driven Decisions with Agentic AI







Instructor
Kerry Back kerry.e.back@rice.edu J. Howard Creekmore Professor of Finance and Professor of Economics






AI agents that understand plain English, query your data, and deliver answers instantly are transforming how organizations make decisions. This eight-session course gives you hands-on experience building and using these tools — no coding background required.
You’ll use AI to wrangle data, generate charts and reports, solve optimization problems, and automate workflows. You’ll learn how to build custom AI agents that connect to databases and documents, how to secure them for enterprise use, and how to develop an implementation plan for your organization. 90 minute Zoom sessions twice weekly for four weeks.
Our AI Lab provides each participant with cloud-based access to Claude Code so you can work with AI tools without installing software or subscribing to an AI provider.



Enroll →


Session 1: The AI Landscape. Chatbots and agents; the major models; large language models from 30,000 feet; Claude Desktop: Chat/Cowork/Code; API calls; prompting techniques.
Session 2: Working with AI. Terminal commands and Python scripts; querying databases; common data operations; generating charts, Excel workbooks, PowerPoint decks, and text output; Claude skills and connectors.
Session 3: Optimization and Prediction with AI. Optimization; exploring tradeoffs between competing objectives; simulation; predictive models; train/test; evaluation of predictive models.
Session 4: Automating Workflows with AI. How software works; building database interfaces and dashboards; automatic table, chart, and document production; containers and deployment.
Session 5: AI Agents and Security. Claude Agent SDK, data processing agreements, prompt injection and data exfiltration defenses; containers and proxies.
Session 6: Deep Dive into Agents. Build teams of agents; use containers to implement least privilege; build a secure personal-assistant agent.
Session 7: Make Your AI Smarter. Retrieval augmented generation; graph databases; fine tuning.
Session 8: Capstone. Pitch your implementation plan and get peer feedback.

,

Online, in progress







Errata







Excel workbook with VBA code Errata Data for an exercise

Working Papers
Back, K., and A. Ober, 2025, Performance of Factor Models in a Simple Economy.
Back, K., Celebi, O., Kakhbod, A., and A. M. Reppen, 2025, Segmented Trading Markets.
Back, K., Cocquemas, F., Ekren, I., and A. Lioui, 2024, Optimal Transport and Informed Trading in Securities Markets.


Recent Publications
Back, K., Carlin, B., Kazempour, S. M., and C. Xie, 2024, Discretionary Announcement Timing and Stock Returns Journal of Finance, forthcoming.
Back, K., Crotty, K., and S. M. Kazempour, 2022, Validity, Tightness, and Forecasting Power of Risk Premium Bounds, Journal of Financial Economics, 144, 732–760.
Back, K., and P. Barton, 2022, Mediation and Strategic Delay in Bargaining and Markets, Journal of Economic Dynamics and Control, 141.
Back, K., Liu, R., and A. Teguia, 2020, Signaling in OTC Markets: Benefits and Costs of Transparency, Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis, 55, 47–75.
Back, K., Liu, R., and A. Teguia, 2019, Increasing Risk Aversion and Life-Cycle Investing Mathematics and Financial Economics, 13, 287–302.
Back, K., Collin-Dufresne, P., Fos, V., Li, T., and A. Ljungqvist, 2018, Activism, Strategic Trading, and Liquidity, Econometrica, 86, 1431–1463.
Back, K., Crane, A., and K. Crotty, 2018, Skewness Consequences of Seeking Alpha, Review of Financial Studies, 31, 4720–4761.
Back, K., Crotty, K., and T. Li, 2018, Identifying Information Asymmetry in Securities Markets, Review of Financial Studies, 31, 2277–2325.
Back, K., and K. Crotty, 2015, The Informational Role of Stock and Bond Volume, Review of Financial Studies, 28, 1381–1427.
Back, K., 2014, A Characterization of the Coskewness-Cokurtosis Pricing Model Economics Letters 125, 219–222.
Back, K., and S. Baruch, 2013, Strategic Liquidity Provision in Limit Order Markets, Econometrica, 81, 363–392.

J. Howard Creekmore Professor of Finance  Jones Graduate School of Business Professor of Economics  School of Social Sciences







Executive Education Course: From BI to AI →

 I teach asset pricing theory to PhD students in the Jones School and the Department of Economics, data-driven finance to students in the Masters of Data Science program in the Department of Computer Science, and generative AI for finance and quantitative investments to MBA students in the Jones School. I am also currently teaching monthly sections of an online executive education course on agentic AI — two online sessions per week for four weeks, linked above. 
Before joining Rice, I served on the faculties of Northwestern University, Indiana University, Washington University in St. Louis, and Texas A&M University. I served as Senior Associate Dean at the Olin School at Washington University in St. Louis. I’m a former editor of the Review of Financial Studies, a former editor of Finance & Stochastics, and a former associate editor of the Journal of Finance and various other academic journals in economics and finance. I’ve written two textbooks and numerous articles in the leading finance and economics journals. My papers received the Best Paper Award at the Review of Financial Studies (1993) and the Best Paper in Corporate Finance Award at the Western Finance Association (2014).
 I served as the Finance Ph.D. Coordinator for the Jones School for 2009 through 2026 and as the Area Coordinator for the finance faculty group for 2023 through 2026. Recently, I’ve been doing research on disclosure policies, security market design, and complexity and momentum in asset pricing. I’ve also been advising several strong Ph.D. students. Among other teaching awards, I received the Financial Management Association Innovation in Teaching Award in 2023 and 2025 and the Jones Graduate School of Business Ph.D. Mentoring Award in 2024 and 2026.

PhD
BUSI 520: Python for Business Research BUSI 521/ECON 505: Asset Pricing Theory/Financial Economics I


MBA
MGMT 638: Gen AI and Quantitative Investing MGMT 675: Generative AI for Finance Python Workshop Short Intro to Python workshop for MBA students


Master of Data Science
BUSI 721: Data-Driven Finance I BUSI 722: Data-Driven Finance II

title: AI Tips for Faculty
text:
If you ask AI to build a slide deck in Beamer, ask it to then generate images of individual slides from the pdf and review them for formatting issues. It can detect overflows from the logs, but it needs to generate images (e.g., pngs) of the slides in order to see other formatting issues (spacing between elements, etc.).
Claude Code (and Gemini CLI and OpenAI Codex) is a universal agent. All you have to do is add instructions/rules/Python scripts/MCP connectors or whatever in Claude.md, Claude skills, or Claude plugins. Then just tell Claude to do things, and it will consult the instructions, etc.
Skills are just text files. A key thing about skills is that Claude only reads them when needed, so they don’t clog up the context window. You can tell Claude to use them with a slash command (/skill_name), or you can describe the skill in the Claude.md file (another text file that Claude always reads on startup), so Claude will use the skill when appropriate without being prompted by a slash command.
You can easily create a Beamer skill. Ask Claude to do it for you. Give it your preferences for theme and styling. You can add rules like: ‘no more than 5 seconds to read,’ ‘no more than 3 bullet points,’ or whatever. Tell it that it should generate slide images whenever it creates a deck and review them for formatting. Or split the tasks if you don’t always need image generation: maybe beamer-create and beamer-review.




If you have an AI CLI tool (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI), lots of possibilities open up. For example, it can search your hard drive to find files with only vague clues - e.g., “I have a docx file somewhere in my dropbox folder that discusses … Find it.”
If you use git, then you can also search the past. Ask Claude to install git, then ask it to initialize a folder as a git repo and commit it. Periodically, tell it “commit changes.” Each time it commits, it is creating a snapshot of the folder. Later, you can say “I used to have a version of this file with a paragraph that discussed … Find the paragraph.” Git is great but a bit complex. Fortunately, AI knows all about it, so we don’t need to know anything.
Asking Claude to search past commits is much easier than searching manually through old Dropbox or Overleaf versions. You can also ask AI to push to GitHub if you want a remote backup - or if you want to share files or create websites using GitHub Pages, etc.




Last fall, Google gave NotebookLM the ability to generate slide decks. Last month, Google added the ability to edit individual slides. If you’ve played around with image generation, you know the drill: ask for one thing to be fixed, and AI makes unwanted changes to two other features in the process. It’s the same with NotebookLM slides. Being able to make changes to one slide at a time reduces the pain, but it is still too painful to work with, IMO, though it does produce some interesting slides. This PDF is a Beamer deck I gave NotebookLM, and this PPTX is NotebookLM’s version (second version, actually - after I told NotebookLM to tone down its first version).
Claude Code created the Beamer deck for me, using the autonomous “write, compile, generate PNG, read and edit, repeat” procedure I described in an earlier email. I asked Claude if it could make the Beamer deck more visually appealing, and it chose to work with Quarto, which produces reveal.js HTML slides. I’ve created those myself in the past. Quarto is a Markdown language, similar in spirit to TeX (and accepts TeX math notation), but it is simpler than TeX and at the same time more versatile - produces websites, online books, can run embedded code to generate visualizations inline, etc. Claude can also use Python to produce PowerPoint decks, but Claude said the output and process would be much better with Quarto. Here is Claude’s output. I think it is an improvement over Beamer and was relatively painless.
To use this process effectively, you need to add an MCP connector for a browser tool to your Claude setup. Claude can do that for you. Then Claude can view the deck itself and make adjustments without prompting. As with Beamer, I told Claude to produce a deck, view and assess, edit the deck, and repeat the process until Claude was happy with the result.
A possible downside of HTML slide decks is that students may be unable to write notes on them. This is solved by enabling Hypothesis in Quarto (ask Claude). When you click the icon at the top right of the slides, you are prompted to sign in to your Hypothesis account (accounts are free). Once you’ve done so, you can annotate the deck just as you would annotate a PDF in Acrobat. When you return to the website (i.e., slide deck) and log back into Hypothesis, your annotations are visible. Apparently, Hypothesis stores a copy of the website with annotations and then serves that copy when you log in.
If you haven’t already tried MCP connectors for mail and calendar apps, then you might want to give it a shot. I have two Gmail accounts and associated calendars (Rice and personal), and Rice’s security policy makes merging them difficult. But the security policy doesn’t restrict API access, so Claude can use them for me. Go figure.
Claude walked me through setting up Google’s MCP connectors. Now, I can say, “Are there any events in my emails that should be added to my calendars? If so, add them”, “Are there any emails that I need to reply to?”, “Draft an email to … about … saying …”
You can also ask Claude to keep a task list for you. Tell it to create a task skill that stores your tasks in categories like personal, teaching, or research. Then, you can ask, “What are my open teaching tasks?”, “Add a task to do …” Obviously, there are many to-do-list apps, and they are easy enough to use, but it simplifies things to reduce the number of apps I use and do everything inside Claude Code (the way all of personal computing will soon be, I think).
Along the same lines, Canvas has an API. You have to click through Canvas and find a link to request an API key from some staff person (Claude can tell you how). Once you have the key, you don’t need to SSO login and two-factor authenticate anymore. Claude can upload files and assignments, download assignment submissions, upload the gradebook and comments, and, of course, help with grading. Ask for a complete summary of why Claude assigned the grades it did, and use that as a starting point for your grading.  
To be FERPA-compliant, ask for a script to upload a dummy gradebook and then run the script on the actual gradebook.  Obviously, you should also anonymize assignments before asking Claude to grade them. 





If you’ve used Claude Code, you’ve probably already encountered subagents. Claude will sometimes spawn them spontaneously when a task can clearly benefit from parallelization. You can also direct Claude to do tasks with subagents. They run in the background, so you can continue your conversation, including asking for more subagents to do more things, all in parallel.
I’ve found this critique skill to be useful. Claude created it at my request. Anthropic recently merged skills and slash commands. You can invoke a skill as a slash command. Here, you would give Claude the prompt /critique <filename>. It spawns three subagents. Each critiques the file (document, slide deck, …) from a different viewpoint and reports back to the main Claude agent, which then reports to you. You can ask Claude to run it in a loop, making recommended changes and critiquing again until no additional changes are needed. If you’ve set Claude to bypass permissions, it will run autonomously.
To use Claude to edit files on jgsrc1 or another remote server, you can install Claude Code at the command line. Execute
curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash
in a terminal window on jgsrc1. Then execute
claude
to open Claude Code on jgsrc1. Instead of running claude in a terminal window, you can use VS Code. Install the Remote SSH and Claude Code extensions for VS Code, remote ssh into jgsrc1 from within VS Code, and then use Claude Code inside VS Code. You’ll be working on jgsrc1 and running Claude Code on jgsrc1 while running VS Code on your own computer.




There has been a lot of chatter online about using Claude Code with the note-taking/organization app Obsidian and also about using Obsidian in conjunction with Andrej Karpathy’s LLM wiki idea. A couple of links are posted below, but just ask Claude if it would be a good idea for you and then ask Claude to implement it.
The benefit of using Obsidian rather than a task skill is minor, I think — Obsidian just gives a visual view of everything. The wiki might be more useful. When I asked Claude how I would use it, I got this response:

The wiki is for me more than for you. When you ask me to help with a research project, I can scan the wiki index to instantly know what papers you have, how they connect, and what’s relevant — without reading 135 PDFs from scratch each time. For example:

“What papers do we have on momentum crashes?” — I check the wiki, find Daniel-Moskowitz 2016, Barroso-Santa-Clara 2015, etc., and summarize what they say.
“How should we position the credit ratings paper relative to existing work?” — I read the relevant wiki notes and draft a literature positioning.
“Write a lit review section for the strategy paper” — I pull from wiki notes tagged to back-crotty-dittmar and draft it.

Your process stays the same: You tell me what you need, and the wiki makes me faster and more accurate at helping. You don’t need to browse the wiki yourself (though you can — it’s all readable markdown in Obsidian with cross-links).
Where it compounds over time:

When you drop in a new paper, I compile a wiki note and link it to your projects
When you have a conversation about a paper with a collaborator, I update the wiki note with your insights
Over months, the wiki accumulates your perspective on the literature, not just abstracts

The wiki is a background layer that makes me a better research assistant.

Links:

How I Took Karpathy’s LLM Wiki and Built an AI-Powered Second Brain in Obsidian
Claude Code + Obsidian: Build the Ultimate AI Knowledge Base

title: Academic Studio
text:
Academic Studio is a bundle of Claude Code, a file browser, a file viewer and editor, and a simplified install method for extensions and supporting software that are especially useful for business professionals, students, and teachers. Claude Code launches on startup.
On first startup, if your computer is not already logged in to Anthropic, you should enter /login in the Claude prompt window to initiate the login process. Open this guide any time from Help → Academic Studio Help.



Claude Code, built in. The Claude Code assistant opens automatically and works alongside your files. Ask it to write, analyze data, build slides, or create documents.
Real documents. Claude can create and edit Excel, Word, PowerPoint, PDF, LaTeX, and Quarto files using its document skills — ask for “a budget spreadsheet” or “a 10-slide deck” and you get an actual file you can open and share.
A workspace, not just a chat. Open a folder and your files and your conversation history live with that project — your work persists.
Python, Jupyter, R, LaTeX, Quarto. Bundled support for data analysis, notebooks, statistics, typesetting, and HTML document creation.
Slides with Quarto + reveal.js. Write a .qmd file with format: revealjs; Claude is great at these, and the plain text stays easy to edit afterward.
One-stop setup. Help → Run Setup… lets you pick your profile (Faculty or Students & Professionals), choose which extensions are active, and install supporting programs (Python, Node.js, Quarto, R, TinyTeX) with one click.
Installation of important Python libraries: the scientific stack plus libraries to create Office documents.




Open a folder: File → Open Folder… — your files appear in the Explorer on the left.
Talk to Claude in the panel that opens on startup — ask it to create a file, write some code, or build a document.
Files save automatically a moment after you stop typing.





Academic Studio runs Claude Code like the Code mode of Claude Desktop. The principal benefits for business professionals and students of Academic Studio relative to Claude Desktop Code are the integrated file browser and file viewer/editor and the one-stop install of Python and Node.js.
For faculty and researchers, the one-click run/build for LaTeX, Quarto, Python, R, and Jupyter are the most important benefits.
Unlike Claude Desktop, Academic Studio does not use point-and-click to install skills, MCP connectors, and plugins. However, there is an easier method — just ask Claude to install them.



Under the hood Academic Studio is VS Code (via the open-source VSCodium), so it will feel familiar if you’ve used VS Code — but it’s simplified for getting work done:

Menus and toolbars are trimmed, with beginner-friendly defaults.
Files auto-save a second after you stop typing (auto-save is off by default in standard VS Code).
Claude Code is built in and opens on startup.
One-stop installation for important tools — Office Viewer, PDF, Quarto, Python, Jupyter, R, LaTeX — so there’s no hunting for extensions.
Help → Run Setup… installs the supporting programs (Python, Quarto, R, TinyTeX, Node.js) for you.
No telemetry and no Copilot; extensions come from the open Open VSX registry.





Command Palette (every command): press F1.
Re-run setup any time from Help → Run Setup… to change your extensions or install more programs.
R IntelliSense needs the R languageserver package: install R, then run install.packages("languageserver") in R.

Command Palette (every command): press F1.
Re-run setup any time from Help → Run Setup… to change your extensions or install more programs.
R IntelliSense needs the R languageserver package: install R, then run install.packages("languageserver") in R.

title: Voiceover
text:
Voiceover turns a PDF slide deck into a narrated MP4 video plus a transcript. It is a Claude Code skill: Claude writes the per-slide narration, you review and revise it — edit it yourself or just tell Claude what to change — then pick an AI voice and generate the video. It is designed for instructors who want to turn lecture slides into self-paced narrated videos, but it works for any slide deck.
Source and issues: github.com/kerryback/skills (the voiceover plugin).



Instructor — runs the skill locally to author the video. Claude writes the narration; the instructor reviews it, picks a voice, and generates.
Students — run nothing. They receive two files, <name>.mp4 (the narrated video) and <name>.txt (the transcript), and view them like any other course material.




Two things you provide; everything else is handled for you.

Quarto on your PATH — renders the deck for the video. Install from quarto.org.
An ElevenLabs API key in ELEVENLABS_API_KEY — the text-to-speech voice. Get a free key at elevenlabs.io.

Handled for you: Python (the first launch builds a small virtual environment), ffmpeg (ships with the Python dependencies), and the web interface (ships prebuilt). No Anthropic API key is needed for the narration — Claude Code writes it. The skill checks for Quarto and the ElevenLabs key on first use and offers to help set them up.



Use any one of these, then invoke it in Claude Code with /voiceover on a PDF slide deck.


npx skills@latest add kerryback/skills
Pick voiceover from the menu (add --list to preview). Installs into ~/.claude/skills/.



/plugin marketplace add kerryback/skills
/plugin install voiceover@kerryback-skills



git clone https://github.com/kerryback/skills.git
ln -s "$(pwd)/skills/plugins/voiceover/skills/voiceover" ~/.claude/skills/voiceover





Launch on a slide deck. Point the skill at a PDF (/voiceover deck.pdf). A small local app opens in your browser on the Narration step.
Claude drafts the narration. Claude reads the deck and writes spoken narration for every slide, one continuous lecture. It appears in the app within a few seconds.
Review and revise. Change the script either way: edit any slide’s text directly in the app (it autosaves), or just tell Claude what you want — “tighten slide 3,” “warmer tone on the intro,” “add a worked example here” — and Claude revises it. Iterate until it reads well.
Pick a voice and generate. On the Generate step, choose an ElevenLabs voice (including your own cloned voices) and build the video.
Preview and share. Watch the result on the Preview step. Each build writes <name>.mp4 and <name>.txt to your project folder, ready to share however you distribute course materials — your LMS, a shared drive, YouTube, or a NotebookLM notebook.

Each deck is saved by name, so you can reopen it later to edit the narration and regenerate the video.



The narration is written by Claude Code from your deck; the app makes no model calls of its own and stores nothing outside your project folder. The finished video and transcript are ordinary files on your machine.
